title: Top lessons to learn from refactoring code with a headless CMS
author: Gary Butler
summary: Background The Harry’s European website (for Harry’s customers in the UK and the rest of Europe), like most websites, is a content-heavy website providing customers with information about our products and services, which means we seek to keep our content management separate from our codebase to enable both to change independently of each other as well as allowing content to…

title: (Forge) Bridge over troubled water
author: John Price
summary: Photo by Chris Becker on Unsplash When we used to think about the process of going from digital design to a fully functional webpage, the word “handoff” frequently came up. The handoff was when a digital designer gave her complete, fully annotated, never-changing, pixel accurate website design to a front-end developer who would recreate her precise design in code. Just…

title: Traditions — Sprint Reviews
author: Showie
summary: Traditions — Sprint Reviews Teams all over the world demo their products every day. Engineering teams, marketing teams, product design teams, a picture speaks a thousand words. People like to see, touch, and feel things. Within the Agile framework these demos are typically part of a Sprint Review which is a formal ceremony within an iteration. Photo by Alex Litvin…

title: Engineering Traditions
author: Showie
summary: Lately I’ve been thinking a lot about traditions in engineering. Why engineering teams do the things they do. In life, we are members of many groups that have their own traditions. Those traditions are often passed down by members of the group and the group is able to sustain them over many years. These groups perpetuate their traditions by celebrating…

title: Planning Harry’s 2019 Hackathon
author: Showie
summary: In December of 2019 Harry’s held its annual Hackathon. title: Why We Migrated Our Engineering Blog From Jekyll to Medium
author: Tim Brown
summary: Goodbye, old friend. (Neat logo though!). When we first launched the Harry’s Engineering Blog back in October of 2013, it was a custom Jekyll application hosted with GitHub Pages.
